mnemonics.title: PTAS Phrase
mnemonics.content: PTAS = 'Please Try Approximate Solution' - Remember: PTAS gives solution within (1-ε) of optimal in poly(n/ε) time

mnemonics.title: FPTAS Fast Four
mnemonics.content: FPTAS = 'Fully Polynomial Time Approximation Scheme' - Remember: FPTAS runs in poly(n, 1/ε), faster for small ε

mnemonics.title: Vertex Cover Victory
mnemonics.content: Vertex Cover: 'Pick Each Edge, Cover Both Ends' - Greedy picks edges, puts both endpoints in cover

mnemonics.title: Set Cover Selects
mnemonics.content: Set Cover: 'Greedy Picks Sets With Greatest Bang For Buck' - Always pick set covering most uncovered elements

mnemonics.title: Two-Approx for Vertex Cover
mnemonics.content: Vertex Cover 2-approx: 'Match and Cover' - Take maximal matching, cover all endpoints = 2-approximation

mnemonics.title: Metric TSP Triangle
mnemonics.content: Metric TSP: 'Twice Around The Tree' - MST traversal gives 2-approximation, triangle inequality holds

mnemonics.title: Knapsack DP Remember
mnemonics.content: Knapsack: 'Dynamic Programming with Size and Value' - FPTAS uses ε-scaling of values

mnemonics.title: Primal Dual Pair
mnemonics.content: Primal-Dual: 'Primal Builds Solution, Dual Provides Lower Bound' - Work together simultaneously

acronyms.usage: Algorithm running in poly(n, 1/ε) time, gives (1-ε)-approximation
Algorithm running in poly(n, 1/ε) time, strongly polynomial in ε

acronyms.expansion: Fully Polynomial Time Randomized Approximation Scheme
acronyms.usage: Randomized version of FPTAS, uses Monte Carlo technique
Find minimum vertices touching all edges - classic 2-approx problem
Cover all elements with minimum sets - greedy gives H(n)-approx
Visit all cities once, return to start - metric version has 2-approx
Relaxation technique: drop integer constraints, round solution
Original NP-hard formulation with integer constraints
Class of problems with constant-factor approximation algorithms
Optimization problems in NP - target for approximation research

visualTips.visualization: Think of a target (optimal) and your arrow (approximation) - ratio shows how close you landed. For minimization: ALG/OPT ≤ ρ, for maximization: OPT/ALG ≤ ρ
Two hands working together - left hand (primal) builds feasible solution, right hand (dual) raises dual variables, they never let go of feasibility
Imagine inflated balloon (LP relaxation) being squeezed back to integer values - some air (approximation error) remains
Filling a basket with biggest available items first - each time pick the set covering most remaining uncovered elements
Build minimum spanning tree, walk twice around it (depth-first), shortcut repeats - gives 2x the optimal tour
Draw edges, find maximal matching (no two edges share vertices), highlight both endpoints of each matched edge - you're covered!
Think of ε as 'error budget' - smaller ε = more accuracy = more computation time

analogies.analogy: Like measuring with a slightly worn ruler - you won't get exact inches but close enough for practical work
PTAS is like having a slow but accurate friend; FPTAS is like having a fast friend who can also do quick estimates
Like packing for a trip - always pick the clothing item that covers the most occasions you'll need outfits for
Like placing security cameras at intersections - each camera covers streets on both sides, you'll double-cover some but catch all
Like planning a house tour by visiting rooms via the hallway backbone - you'll walk some corridors twice but visit every room
Like taking a photo with soft focus (relaxed integers) then sharpening (rounding) - some details get lost in translation
Like negotiation - primal asks 'what's the minimum I can accept?', dual asks 'what's the maximum I can pay?' - they meet in the middle
Like pricing items then rounding to nearest Rs.10 - you lose some precision but calculation becomes much faster
